title: End of module: Fields 1: Gravitational and Electric Fields

questions.text: Two point masses of $5\,\text{kg}$ and $8\,\text{kg}$ have their centres $2\,\text{m}$ apart. Using $G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{N m}^2\text{kg}^{-2}$, calculate the gravitational force between them, in newtons.

A planet has mass $6.0 \times 10^{24}\,\text{kg}$ and radius $6.4 \times 10^{6}\,\text{m}$. Using $G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{N m}^2\text{kg}^{-2}$, calculate the gravitational field strength at its surface, in N kg$^{-1}$.

A satellite orbits Earth geostationarily with period $T = 24$ hours $= 86400\,\text{s}$. Given $GM_{\text{Earth}} = 3.98 \times 10^{14}\,\text{m}^3\text{s}^{-2}$, use $r^3 = \dfrac{GM\,T^2}{4\pi^2}$ to calculate the orbital radius $r$ in metres.
Which of these correctly defines gravitational field strength $g$?
choices: The force per unit charge experienced by a test charge
The force per unit mass experienced by a test mass
The potential energy per unit mass at a point
The rate of change of gravitational potential with time
questions.explanation: By definition $g = F/m$: the gravitational force per unit mass experienced by a small test mass placed at that point.

questions.text: Two point charges of $+4\,\mu\text{C}$ and $+6\,\mu\text{C}$ are $0.3\,\text{m}$ apart. Using $k = 8.99 \times 10^{9}\,\text{N m}^2\text{C}^{-2}$, calculate the magnitude of the force between them, in newtons.
Two point charges repel one another. Which statement must be true?
choices: Both charges have the same sign
One of the charges must be zero
questions.explanation: Like charges repel and unlike charges attract, so a repulsive force tells us only that the two charges share the same sign — both could be positive or both could be negative.

questions.text: Calculate the electric field strength at a distance of $0.2\,\text{m}$ from a point charge of $+5\,\text{nC}$. Use $k = 8.99 \times 10^{9}\,\text{N m}^2\text{C}^{-2}$. Give your answer in N C$^{-1}$.
Two parallel plates $0.05\,\text{m}$ apart have a potential difference of $2000\,\text{V}$ across them. Calculate the electric field strength between the plates, in V m$^{-1}$.
questions.explanation: For a uniform field between parallel plates, $E = \dfrac{V}{d} = \dfrac{2000}{0.05} = 40{,}000\,\text{V m}^{-1}$.

questions.text: Which of the following are true of gravitational fields but **not necessarily** true of electric fields?
choices: The field obeys an inverse-square law for a point source
The potential is always negative (taking infinity as the zero of potential)
questions.explanation: Gravity is always attractive and gravitational potential is always negative because mass is always positive. Electric fields can be repulsive and electric potential can be positive or negative, depending on the sign of the source charge. The inverse-square law itself is shared by both, so it does not distinguish them.

questions.text: Give the SI unit of gravitational field strength (equivalently, of acceleration).
questions.explanation: Gravitational field strength $g = F/m$ has SI unit newtons per kilogram, $\text{N kg}^{-1}$, which is dimensionally identical to metres per second squared, $\text{m s}^{-2}$.
